envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers include manufacturers, distributors, government agencies, freight forwarders, movers, exporters, art and equipment owners, and other shippers. Revenue is commonly job-, unit-, material-, labor-, or requirements-contract based, including fixed-price work, scheduled repeat services, call-off orders, and customer-specific recurring arrangements.
lens.excluded_activities: Packaging and labeling services classified in 561910, in-house or captive shipper and warehouse packing departments, primary freight carriage or warehousing activities, one-person shells without transferable operations, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms that repeatedly provide outsourced packing, custom or standard crating, protective preparation, marking, weighing, documentation, and related preparation of customer goods for transportation.

a.bridge: BLS reports the broader 4889 Other Support Activities for Transportation occupation mix, combining 488991 with 488999, rather than a 488991-only wage-weighted task distribution. O*NET tasks and BLS/NIST automation evidence are mapped qualitatively to current AI, computer vision, optimization, and AI-enabled robotics. The estimate is adjusted downward because 488999 likely contributes disproportionate customer-service and administrative employment; it may still understate exposure where standardized packing cells can use machine vision and robots, or overstate it where custom crates and irregular high-value goods require dexterous manual judgment.
caveats: The occupation mix covers NAICS 4889 and includes activities outside packing and crating.
O*NET tasks cover packers across all industries and do not provide 488991 task time or payroll weights.
BLS projections largely reflect retail, warehouse, and manufacturing packers, whose automation economics may differ from outsourced custom crating.

The injected compensation ratio is unusually low relative to the physically labor-intensive occupation evidence and may reflect gross receipts, material or pass-through revenue, vintage mismatch, and cross-code harmonization.
a.change_evidence: Obtain a 488991-only OEWS special tabulation with payroll by occupation and conduct time-and-motion studies across at least 20 LMM custom-crating, export-packing, government-contract, and household-goods packing firms, documenting existing machinery and task variability.
a.rationale: The proxy occupation mix is led by hand packers, material movers, drivers, supervisors, and equipment operators, and O*NET shows core work in inspection, measuring, weighing, sealing, crate assembly, padding, moving, and labeling. Exposed work is concentrated in quote preparation, dimensional capture, material and crate-design recommendations, scheduling, work instructions, inventory and shipping records, customer communication, label generation, and vision-assisted inspection. Some repetitive physical handling is exposed to AI-enabled automation, but variable low-volume custom work limits the not-yet-automated share.

d5.bridge: BTS forecasts total US freight tonnage, not purchased packing-and-crating service quantity. Its 50% tonnage growth from 2020 to 2050 implies roughly 1.4% annual compound growth and about 1.07 over five years; the base is reduced to 1.05 for self-packing, packaging standardization, and automation. The proxy may understate outsourced demand if high-value, fragile, export, defense, art, and specialized machinery shipments grow faster than tonnage, or overstate it if bulk freight dominates growth and shippers insource preparation.
caveats: Aggregate freight tonnage is an indirect driver and includes bulk commodities requiring little or no outsourced packing.
The BTS forecast is long term and based on an older FAF vintage rather than a five-year 488991 forecast.
A recession or trade shock at the year-five endpoint could materially reduce specialized crating demand.
d5.change_evidence: Construct an activity-weighted forecast using 488991 company job counts, crate units and cubic volume, FPDS packing/crating obligations, machinery and equipment shipments, export container data, art-logistics volumes, and customer make-versus-buy plans, separating price and materials inflation from service quantity.
d5.rationale: Goods still must be protected and prepared for transportation, and long-run freight activity is forecast to grow. Constant-price demand should expand modestly rather than track shipment value because much freight does not require specialist crating and customers can standardize, reuse packaging, or pack internally. Industrial cycles, trade, defense procurement, capital-goods shipments, relocations, and construction create a wide range around the base.

caveats: No source reports eligibility under the frozen lens.
The injected count of 45 firms is margin-bridged using a broad transportation margin that may not fit material-intensive custom crating.
Establishment classification can be blurred when packing is bundled with moving, freight forwarding, warehousing, or manufactured packaging.
e.change_evidence: Build a verified 488991 firm census from Census microdata, SAM and FPDS, commercial databases, state registrations, and company websites, then classify external-customer share, repeat revenue, facility and equipment ownership, customer concentration, captive status, and transferability.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition itself centers on preparing customers' goods for transportation, so most active LMM firms should provide an external service with transferable equipment, facilities, crews, procedures, customer relationships, and contracts. Eligibility is reduced by businesses that are effectively captive adjuncts to moving, warehousing, or manufacturing; operations reliant on a founder's personal relationships or craft expertise; project-only firms without repeat demand; and entities whose normalized EBITDA is distorted by materials or subcontractor pass-through.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 488991 ($1,153,775,074) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 488991 ($25,593,316,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0983 (margins.json[48]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Transportation EBITDA/Sales 9.83%); revenue/employee $1,113,334 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 488991); class <5: 769 firms, avg revenue/firm $526,502 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $51,755 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 311 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,115,376 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $109,641 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 177 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,072,079 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $301,985 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 126 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,618,873 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $650,635 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 45 firms, avg revenue/firm $19,374,933 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,904,556 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 24 firms, avg revenue/firm $941,331,083 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $92,532,845 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 45

caveats: Defense household-goods requirements illustrate accountability but not the full industrial and commercial mix.
Robotics progress could accelerate for repeated standard goods while remaining limited for one-off crates.
An automated packing company still counts as an accountable operator under this primitive.
o.change_evidence: Survey repeat customers by shipment type on planned insourcing and automation, and measure the share of jobs converted to unattended packing cells, reusable standard containers, supplier-direct packaging, or software-only instructions in deployments operating for at least 12 months.
o.rationale: The service produces a physical, damage-preventing outcome: inspecting goods, selecting materials, building and padding crates, packing, marking, weighing, documenting, and accepting responsibility. Software can design, quote, document, and guide work, and robots can handle some standardized items, but neither eliminates the accountable operator for most custom, fragile, irregular, regulated, or high-value goods. Some simple work can be self-served or folded into automated shipper and warehouse operations, preventing a value of one.

caveats: The GAO procurement evidence concerns one Navy competition and is not representative of all commercial accounts.
Material prices and damage claims can obscure retained labor productivity.
The estimate excludes implementation difficulty, demand changes, and valuation effects.
q.change_evidence: Review a stratified sample of 100 quotes, contracts, change orders, and renewals for fixed-price, unit-rate, time-and-materials, cost-plus, escalation, benchmarking, rebid, and open-book terms; compare realized margins and prices after documented productivity changes.
q.rationale: Fixed-price jobs and requirements contracts allow labor, material-yield, quoting, and rework savings to remain with the operator until repricing, and customer-specific craftsmanship makes internal process economics imperfectly visible. Retention is reduced by competitive bids and reverse auctions, transparent labor and material components, customer rebids, cost-plus arrangements, and buyers' ability to compare quotes or perform simple packing internally. The five-year discounted base retains somewhat more than half of implemented benefit.

caveats: Automation feasibility varies sharply between repeated standard products and one-off irregular or fragile goods.
NIST robotics evidence covers manufacturing and fulfillment applications rather than 488991 firms.
Economy-wide AI adoption does not measure successful workflow implementation in packing and crating.
This primitive excludes demand, pricing, and valuation effects.
rho.change_evidence: Collect before-and-after labor hours, quote cycle time, material yield, damage claims, throughput, override rates, and capital costs from LMM deployments of 3D scanning, generative crate design, computer-vision inspection, cobots, robotic picking, and automated documentation for at least 12 months.
rho.rationale: Document intake, estimating, basic crate design, materials planning, scheduling, inventory creation, labels, customer updates, and visual inspection assistance are implementable with current tools. Physical automation is more constrained: NIST describes robotic picking as difficult for randomly placed, hard-to-recognize or hard-to-grasp objects, with loose and complex packaging remaining challenging. LMM capital constraints, small batches, changing goods, space, integration, safety, and operator training should limit implementation to roughly half of exposed opportunity in the base case.

caveats: The SBA retirement evidence is old, economy-wide, and measures expected retirement rather than business transfers.
Private small-company transactions are underreported.
Sale of equipment or customer relationships without an operating-company transfer is excluded.
s5.change_evidence: Match five years of PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, BizBuySell, SBA 7(a), FPDS novations, UCC filings, and state corporate records to a verified 488991 firm list; supplement with owner-age, succession-plan, and likely-exit interviews across at least 50 owners.
s5.rationale: Small owner-operated service firms generate succession and strategic-sale opportunities, and repeat government or industrial accounts can be transferable. The base remains conservative because no 488991 transfer series was found, small-business owners may retire later than employees, and exits can occur through closure, asset liquidation, family succession, or customer-book loss rather than a qualifying control transfer.

principal_driver: A durable need to physically protect irregular and valuable goods, supplemented by automatable estimating, documentation, planning, and inspection workflows.
principal_weakness: The addressable labor layer is small relative to physical execution, and the injected compensation ratio is both low and potentially distorted by receipts and material pass-through.
